topics.title: Reflection API & Annotations
topics.description: Reflection allows inspecting and modifying classes, methods, fields, and constructors at runtime — even private members. It's the backbone of every Java framework (Spring, Hibernate, Jackson). Custom annotations are metadata markers that can be retained at runtime (RUNTIME) or compile time (SOURCE/CLASS). Annotation processors and reflection together power dependency injection, ORM mapping, JSON serialization, and AOP.
topics.story: Normally code is like a recipe card — fixed instructions you follow. Reflection is like a food scientist who can examine the recipe card itself at runtime: read what ingredients are listed, modify the quantities, even create a new dish from scratch by just knowing the recipe name. Spring's IoC container does exactly this — it reads your @Autowired annotations via reflection and wires dependencies without you writing a single new MyService() call.
topics.analogy: Class.forName() = looking up a recipe by name in a cookbook. getDeclaredFields() = listing all ingredients on the card. setAccessible(true) = overriding the 'secret recipe' lock. Annotation = sticky note on the recipe card with metadata. @Retention(RUNTIME) = sticky note that survives photocopying. @Target = which part of the recipe the note can be stuck to.
topics.memoryAnchor: Class<?> clz = Class.forName('com.Foo') or obj.getClass() or Foo.class. getMethods() = public incl inherited. getDeclaredMethods() = all on this class. setAccessible(true) bypasses access control. invoke(instance, args). Annotation: @interface. @Retention(RUNTIME/CLASS/SOURCE). @Target(FIELD/METHOD/TYPE). getAnnotation(MyAnn.class). getDeclaredAnnotations(). Processor: AbstractProcessor at compile time.
topics.firstPrinciples: The JVM loads classes as Class objects that describe their structure. Reflection is just reading those Class objects at runtime. Annotations are syntactic sugar that attach metadata maps to class elements — they don't do anything by themselves; frameworks read them via reflection and act on the metadata.
topics.bruteForce: Without reflection/annotations: hard-coded wiring — MyService svc = new MyServiceImpl(); Every dependency must be manually constructed and passed. Changing an implementation requires modifying every call site. No convention-over-configuration. ORM requires manual ResultSet → POJO mapping for every field.
topics.optimizedApproach: With reflection + annotations: @Autowired MyService svc; Spring scans classpath, finds @Service classes, instantiates them via reflection, reads @Autowired, and injects dependencies. Jackson reads @JsonProperty field annotations via reflection to serialize/deserialize. Hibernate maps @Column annotations to SQL columns. All at runtime with zero manual wiring.
topics.whenToUse: Use Reflection when: building frameworks/libraries, writing serializers/deserializers, dependency injection containers, test frameworks (JUnit uses reflection to find @Test methods), code generation tools. Avoid reflection in hot paths (performance overhead — cache Method/Field references). Use annotations when: you need to add metadata without changing behavior, configuration in code (vs XML), code generation triggers.

examples.title: Reflection — Inspect and Invoke at Runtime
examples.explanation: getDeclaredMethods() includes private methods of this class. getMethods() includes all inherited public methods. setAccessible(true) bypasses access control (use sparingly — opens security vulnerabilities). invoke(instance, args) calls the method. getDeclaredConstructor().newInstance() creates an object without knowing the type at compile time — this is how Spring creates beans.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring BeanFactory creates beans via getDeclaredConstructor().newInstance(). Jackson uses getDeclaredFields() + setAccessible(true) to map JSON ↔ POJO. JUnit's @Test discovery: getDeclaredMethods() + method.isAnnotationPresent(Test.class). Lombok generates code by processing annotations at compile time (SOURCE retention).

examples.title: Custom Runtime Annotation — Simple Validation Framework
examples.explanation: @Retention(RUNTIME) means the annotation survives compilation and is visible via reflection. @Target restricts where the annotation can be placed. isAnnotationPresent() checks at runtime. getAnnotation().value() reads annotation attributes. This is exactly how Jakarta Bean Validation (@NotNull, @Min) works under the hood — Hibernate Validator is the reference implementation.
examples.realWorldUse: Jakarta Validation: @NotBlank, @Size, @Pattern on DTO fields. JPA: @Entity, @Column, @OneToMany — Hibernate reads these to generate SQL schema. Jackson: @JsonIgnore, @JsonProperty. Spring: @Autowired, @Transactional, @RequestMapping. All use reflection + runtime annotations.

problems.title: Find All @Deprecated Methods
problems.description: Write a method `findDeprecatedMethods(Class<?> clz)` that returns a list of method names in the given class that are annotated with @Deprecated.
problems.hint1: Use clz.getDeclaredMethods() to get all methods.
problems.hint3: Collect matching method names into a List<String>.

problems.title: Simple JSON Serializer Using Reflection
problems.description: Write `toJson(Object obj)` that uses reflection to serialize a simple POJO into a JSON string. Only handle String, int, and boolean field types. Format: {"fieldName":"value",...}
problems.hint2: Check field type: String → quote the value, int/boolean → no quotes.
problems.hint3: Join fields with comma, wrap in {}.

problems.title: Custom @Log Annotation Method Timer
problems.description: Define a @Log annotation with RUNTIME retention and TARGET METHOD. Write a proxy/wrapper that intercepts calls to methods annotated with @Log and prints '[MethodName] executed in Xms'. For simplicity, wrap one specific method call manually.
problems.hint2: Before invoke(), record System.currentTimeMillis(). After, subtract to get duration.
